sync.title: Sync & CRDT System
sync.subtitle: Deep dive into how haex-vault synchronizes data across devices using Column-Level CRDTs and Hybrid Logical Clocks.

overview.title: Sync Overview
overview.intro: haex-vault uses a CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) based sync system that enables seamless offline-first operation with automatic conflict resolution.

offline.title: Offline-First
offline.description: Work without internet. Changes sync when you're back online.

realtime.title: Realtime Sync
realtime.description: A shared WebSocket to haex-sync-server triggers a debounced pull whenever another device pushes.

e2e.title: E2E Encrypted
e2e.description: Every column value is encrypted with the vault key before it leaves the device.

crdtBasics.title: CRDT Fundamentals
crdtBasics.intro: CRDTs are data structures that can be replicated across multiple computers, modified independently, and merged without conflicts.

lww.title: Last-Write-Wins (LWW)
lww.description: haex-vault uses LWW semantics with Hybrid Logical Clocks. The change with the highest HLC always wins, ensuring deterministic conflict resolution across all devices.

columns.title: CRDT Columns
columns.description: Every synced table has two special columns that enable CRDT functionality:
columns.comment: All synced tables get these columns
columns.hlc: Row-level HLC (max across all column HLCs, for efficient queries)
columns.columnHlcs: JSON object with HLC per column
columns.deletesNote: Deletes are tracked in a separate haex_deleted_rows table - there is no tombstone column.

hlc.title: Hybrid Logical Clocks
hlc.intro: HLCs combine physical time with a stable node ID to create globally unique, monotonically increasing timestamps without requiring synchronized clocks.
hlc.benefit1: Lexicographically sortable - can use simple string comparison
hlc.benefit2: Globally unique - the 16-byte node ID prevents collisions

columnLevel.title: Column-Level Sync
columnLevel.intro: Unlike row-level sync, haex-vault tracks changes at the column level. Concurrent edits to different columns of the same row merge automatically without data loss.

important.title: No Data Loss
important.description: With column-level tracking, two devices can edit different fields of the same record simultaneously, and both changes will be preserved.

pkWarning.title: Primary Keys Are NOT Tracked
pkWarning.description: CRDT only tracks non-primary-key columns. If your table has a composite primary key (e.g., PRIMARY KEY (item_id, group_id)) and you update one of the PK columns, that change will NOT sync. Use a single-column PK and keep changeable fields as regular columns.

syncFlow.title: Sync Flow
syncFlow.intro: The sync process has three pieces: push, pull, and a shared WebSocket that signals other devices.

push.title: Push Operation
push.description: When you make changes locally, the sync orchestrator scans dirty tables, groups column-level changes by HLC, encrypts each value, and sends chunks (<= 2000 changes) to the server.

pull.title: Pull Operation
pull.description: Pull fetches server changes since the last HLC cursor. Each change is decrypted and applied with column-level HLC comparison; only newer values win.

realtime.title: Realtime Updates
realtime.intro: haex-vault opens a single shared WebSocket to haex-sync-server, authenticated with the device's DID. Incoming events are used purely as triggers - the authoritative data path is always the pull endpoint.

fallback.title: Fallback Polling
fallback.description: If the WebSocket connection drops (mobile Doze mode, network changes, ...), the orchestrator falls back to periodic pulls and reconnects with exponential backoff.

softDelete.title: Deletes
softDelete.intro: haex-vault deletes rows for real - but a BEFORE DELETE trigger logs the deleted row's table and primary keys into haex_deleted_rows so the deletion can be replicated.
softDelete.why: This means your queries don't need a `WHERE haex_tombstone = 0` filter. Rows that exist are alive; rows that were deleted are gone locally and propagate via haex_deleted_rows to other devices.

events.title: Sync Events
events.intro: After a successful pull, events are emitted automatically by the sync orchestrator to notify the application that data has changed. Extensions don't need to emit these events - they only need to listen and react to them.

automatic.title: Automatic Event Emission
automatic.description: The sync orchestrator emits events after every successful pull. Extensions don't need to emit these events themselves - haex-vault handles it.

twoEvents.title: Two Event Types
twoEvents.internal: For internal Pinia stores (registered via registerStoreForTables)
twoEvents.extensions: For extensions via vault-sdk (use this in your extension)

filtering.title: Permission-Based Event Filtering
filtering.intro: For security and privacy, sync events are filtered based on each extension's database permissions. Extensions only receive notifications about tables they have access to.
filtering.description: When sync completes, haex-vault checks each extension's DB permissions and only includes table names that match:
filtering.prefix: Prefix match (e.g., publicKey__extName__*) - sees tables with that prefix
filtering.exact: Exact match - sees only that specific table
filtering.privacy: This prevents extensions from observing activity in other extensions' tables, ensuring data privacy between extensions.

usage.title: Handling Sync Events in Extensions
usage.intro: The event payload includes a list of table names that were updated (filtered to only tables your extension can access). Check if any of your tables are in the list and reload data accordingly.
